name: tldr
description: Single-line takeaways — the deck or section's headline points.
purpose: Use at the end of a section or deck to restate the takeaways in one line each. Each line should be a complete claim, not a category label.

title.description: Slide heading — typically 'In summary' or 'What this means'.

lines.description: One line per takeaway. Keep each under ~15 words.

skeleton:
<!-- _class: tldr -->

## What this section showed.

- The first takeaway as a complete one-line claim.
- The second takeaway as a complete one-line claim.
- The third takeaway as a complete one-line claim.
- The fourth takeaway as a complete one-line claim.
sample:
<!-- _class: tldr -->

## What this section will tell you, in five lines.

- Components stay short — `cards-grid` not `inventory.grid.cards`.
- The four layers organise the catalog; they do not name components.
- Manifests are the single source of truth for every component.
- Discovery happens via the scaffolder and IDE snippets, not the directive.
- Forty-five components ship — one folder each.

whenToUse.title: Section or deck recap
whenToUse.body: Use at the close of a section or the end of the deck to restate what the room should walk out remembering. The audience reads it as 'if you forget everything else, remember this.'

whenToUse.title: Complete one-line claims
whenToUse.body: Each line is a full claim the audience could quote back — not a category label or a bullet of jargon. If a line needs context, split it into two slides or pick a richer layout.

whenToUse.title: Four to six lines
whenToUse.body: Sweet spot is five. Past six the recap stops feeling like a tldr and starts feeling like a list. Trim or split across two tldr slides.

antiPatterns.title: Category labels, not claims
antiPatterns.body: "Pricing" or "Architecture" wastes the slot — the audience can't act on a label. Each line is the claim itself: "Pricing simplifies to three tiers," not "Pricing."

antiPatterns.title: Wall of long lines
antiPatterns.body: Past about fifteen words the line stops scanning and the recap reads as paragraph soup. Trim ruthlessly or move the longer item to its own subtopic slide.

antiPatterns.title: Mid-deck use
antiPatterns.body: tldr earns its weight at the end. In the middle of a deck it pre-empts the section it's recapping. Use subtopic to open a section and tldr to close it.

related.name: list
related.when: lines are general bullets, not section takeaways

related.name: principles
related.when: each line is a stated rule with a justification body

related.name: closing
related.when: the slide is the deck's final word, not a section recap

related.name: agenda
related.when: previewing what's coming at the top of the deck

numbered.label: Numbered takeaways
numbered.caption: Authored as `ol` (`1.` source). Adds a `01.`, `02.` counter prefix to each line — useful when the recap doubles as a numbered set the audience can reference back to.
numbered.sample:
<!-- _class: tldr numbered -->

## Five takeaways from this section.

1. Components stay short — `cards-grid` not `inventory.grid.cards`.
2. The four layers organise the catalog; they do not name components.
3. Manifests are the single source of truth for every component.
4. Discovery happens via the scaffolder and IDE snippets, not the directive.
5. Forty-five components ship — one folder each.
